skill.name: pptx-official
skill.description: Create, edit, inspect, validate, and convert PowerPoint presentations using PPTX, OOXML, HTML, and helper scripts.
skill.summary: PowerPoint workflow support for creating, editing, analyzing, and validating .pptx presentations.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static scan produced many duplicate hits, and several high labels are false positives from OOXML schema vocabulary, namespace URLs, documentation examples, and fixed command strings. However, the skill includes helper scripts that extract user-supplied Office archives and run external converters, plus instructions to install dependencies with sudo. I found no prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent, but the confirmed operational risks make this unsafe to publish without review and sandboxing guidance.

high_findings.title: Unsafe Office Archive Extraction
high_findings.description: The scripts extract user-supplied Office ZIP containers with extractall without visible path traversal checks. A crafted PPTX, DOCX, or XLSX could write files outside the intended temporary or output directory.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static finding is confirmed by direct extractall calls on Office archives supplied through workflow inputs. I did not find path normalization or member validation at these locations.

high_findings.title: Privileged Dependency Installation Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users or agents to install LibreOffice and Poppler with sudo apt-get. This is not malicious by itself, but marketplace skills should not encourage privileged system changes without explicit approval and isolation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo commands are present in dependency instructions and could be executed by an agent following the workflow. The intent appears to be setup guidance, so this is high operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: External Converters Run on User Documents
medium_findings.description: The helper scripts call fixed external tools such as soffice, pdftoppm, and git. The argument arrays reduce command injection risk, but converting untrusted Office files can still expose parser and converter attack surface.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are real, but they use fixed executable names and argument arrays rather than shell interpolation. The remaining concern is exposure to external document parsers and local tool behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local HTML Rendering Executes Active Content
medium_findings.description: The HTML to PPTX workflow launches Chromium and opens a local file URL. If untrusted HTML is rendered, scripts or external resources in that file could execute in the browser context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly opens local HTML in Chromium, but normal use appears to render agent-generated slide HTML. The risk increases if user-supplied HTML is passed into the workflow.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Use Appears Limited to Temporary Directory Selection
low_findings.description: The process.env finding is a low-risk use of TMPDIR for browser temporary files. I found no evidence that secrets are read or transmitted at this location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line reads TMPDIR with a fallback to /tmp, which is a common configuration pattern. No evidence found of credential access or network exfiltration tied to this environment variable use.

low_findings.title: Schema and Namespace URLs Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Many network findings point to OOXML namespace constants and XSD schema declarations, not runtime network requests. These should be treated as documentation or validation metadata rather than outbound communication.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain namespace URI strings and schema imports. I found no evidence that these specific lines initiate network connections.

low_findings.title: Scanner Blocker Labels Include OOXML Vocabulary False Positives
low_findings.description: Several C2, network scanning, weak cryptography, and path traversal labels match ordinary OOXML schema terms or relative schemaLocation values. These are not evidence of malicious behavior by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review showed benign schema enumeration names, OOXML element names, and relative schema imports at representative flagged lines. This supports dismissing those static labels as false positives in the schema files.

dangerous_patterns.title: zipfile.extractall on User-Controlled Office Archives
dangerous_patterns.description: Archive extraction is performed directly on Office files. This pattern should be replaced with member-by-member extraction that rejects absolute paths and parent directory traversal.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and occurs in workflows intended for user-provided Office documents. The risk is well-known for ZIP-based formats when extraction paths are not checked.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged System Package Installation in Skill Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The dependency section includes sudo apt-get commands. Marketplace review should require safer setup instructions or explicit human approval before any privileged command is run.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct and privileged, but they install common document conversion tools. This is a dangerous operational pattern, not proof of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Document Conversion Processes
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts invoke local conversion tools on presentation files. These commands should run in a sandbox because Office parsers and converters process complex untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static external command findings are confirmed, but command injection is unlikely because shell execution is not used in the reviewed subprocess calls. The main risk is converter exposure.

content.value_statement: Building and reviewing PowerPoint files can require direct PPTX, XML, image, and conversion workflows. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical steps for creating, editing, validating, and visually checking presentations.
actual_capabilities: Creates new .pptx presentations from HTML layouts using pptxgenjs and the included html2pptx workflow.
Extracts and inspects raw OOXML files for slides, notes, comments, themes, layouts, masters, and media.
Edits presentation text, layout, styling, and embedded assets through structured PPTX archive workflows.
Validates Office document structure with included OOXML validation scripts and schema references.
Converts slides to PDF or images for visual review with LibreOffice and Poppler-based commands.
Builds thumbnail sheets to compare slide formatting and detect hidden slides.
limitations: Requires external tools such as LibreOffice, Poppler, Playwright, sharp, markitdown, and pptxgenjs for full workflows.
Some scripts unpack user-supplied Office files and should only run on trusted documents or in a sandbox.
The skill provides workflow guidance and helper scripts, not a hosted presentation service.
Complex animations, proprietary PowerPoint effects, and exact rendering parity may still need manual review.

use_cases.title: Create polished slide decks
use_cases.description: Generate a new presentation with consistent layout, visual hierarchy, typography, colors, and speaker-ready content.

use_cases.title: Audit existing presentations
use_cases.description: Inspect text, notes, comments, layouts, masters, themes, hidden slides, and visual output before delivery.

use_cases.title: Repair or transform PPTX files
use_cases.description: Unpack OOXML, edit structured files, repack the presentation, and validate the result after targeted changes.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize a Presentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pptx-official workflow to extract the text from this presentation and summarize the main message, slide by slide.

prompt_templates.title: Create a New Deck
prompt_templates.scenario: Generate a complete presentation from content
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 10-slide PowerPoint deck about [topic] for [audience]. Choose a suitable visual style, explain the design approach, and generate the PPTX.

prompt_templates.title: Edit an Existing Deck
prompt_templates.scenario: Modify structure and content while preserving design
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this PPTX by replacing outdated metrics, adding speaker notes, and preserving the existing theme, fonts, and layout system.

prompt_templates.title: Validate and Diagnose OOXML
prompt_templates.prompt: Unpack this PPTX, inspect the relevant OOXML files, identify structural or formatting problems, repair them, repack the file, and validate the result.

output_examples.input: Create a six-slide board update from a written project summary.
output: A finished PowerPoint deck with title, agenda, status, risks, timeline, and next steps slides.
A short explanation of the visual direction, color palette, and layout choices.
A validation note describing any external tools used during generation.

output_examples.input: Review an uploaded sales presentation for consistency issues.
output: A slide-by-slide findings list covering inconsistent typography, hidden slides, missing notes, and layout drift.
Thumbnail output guidance for visual comparison.

output_examples.input: Repair a presentation with broken comments and notes.
output: A diagnosis of the relevant OOXML parts and relationships.
A repaired PPTX with preserved slide content.
A validation summary showing whether the package structure is well formed.

best_practices: Run archive and conversion workflows in a sandbox when handling untrusted PPTX, DOCX, or XLSX files.
Analyze the theme, slide masters, layouts, and sample slides before editing an existing presentation.
Validate and visually inspect generated files before sending them to end users.
anti_patterns: Do not run privileged dependency installation commands from the skill without explicit user approval.
Do not extract Office archives from unknown sources into sensitive directories.
Do not assume HTML rendering will perfectly match PowerPoint without thumbnail or PDF review.

faq.question: Can this skill create PowerPoint files from scratch?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes an HTML to PPTX workflow using pptxgenjs and helper scripts for accurate slide positioning.

faq.question: Can it edit an existing presentation?
faq.answer: Yes. It can unpack a PPTX archive, inspect OOXML parts, modify content, and repack the file.

faq.question: Does it support visual review?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes workflows for converting slides to PDF, JPEG images, and thumbnail sheets.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: Full functionality may require Python, Node.js, pptxgenjs, Playwright, sharp, LibreOffice, Poppler, markitdown, and defusedxml.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with untrusted files?
faq.answer: Use caution. Several scripts unpack Office archives and invoke external converters, so sandboxing is recommended.

faq.question: Does it guarantee exact PowerPoint rendering?
faq.answer: No. It can get close, but complex layouts, fonts, animations, and proprietary effects may require manual review.
